title: Answer Set Counting and its Applications
authors: Mohimenul KabirNational University of SingaporeSchool of ComputingSingapore
abstract: We have focused on Answer Set Programming (ASP), more specifically, answer set counting, exploring both exact and approximate methodologies. We developed an exact ASP counter, sharpASP, which utilizes a compact encoding for propositional formulas, significantly enhancing efficiency compared to existing methods that often struggle with inefficient encodings. Our evaluations indicate that sharpASP outperforms current ASP counters on several benchmarks. In addition, we proposed an approximate ASP counter, named ApproxASP, a hashing-based counter integrating Gauss-Jordan elimination within the ASP solver, clingo. As a practical application, we employed ApproxASP for network reliability estimation, demonstrating superior performance over both traditional reliability estimators and #SAT-based methods.

paragraphs: Answer Set Programming (ASP) [23] has emerged as a promising paradigm in knowledge representation and automated reasoning owing to its ability to model hard combinatorial problems from diverse domains in a natural way [4]. Building on advances in propositional SAT solving, the past two decades have witnessed the emergence of well-engineered systems for solving the answer set satisfiability problem, i.e., finding models or answer sets for a given answer set program. In recent years, there has been growing interest in problems beyond satisfiability, such as model counting, in the context of ASP. In this work, we focus on the model counting problem in the context of ASP, known as answer set counting problem. There has been growing interest in answer set counting, motivated by applications in probabilistic reasoning and network reliability [20, 2, 13]
An answer set program PP consists of a set of rules, each rule is structured as follows:

where, a1,вҖҰ,ak,b1,вҖҰ,bm,c1,вҖҰ,cna_{1},\ldots,a_{k},b_{1},\ldots,b_{m},c_{1},\ldots,c_{n} are propositional variables or atoms, and k,m,nk,m,n are non-negative integers. The notations рқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Rules}(P) and and вҖӢP\and{P} denote the rules and atoms within the program PP. In rule rr, the operator вҖңnotвҖқ denotes default negation [6]. For each rule rr (eq. 1), we adopt the following notations: the atom set {a1,вҖҰ,ak}\{a_{1},\ldots,a_{k}\} constitutes the head of rr, denoted by рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r)\mathsf{Head}(r), the set {b1,вҖҰ,bm}\{b_{1},\ldots,b_{m}\} is referred to as the positive body atoms of rr, denoted by рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)+\mathsf{Body}(r)^{+}, and the set {c1,вҖҰ,cn}\{c_{1},\ldots,c_{n}\} is referred to as the negative body atoms of rr, denoted by рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)вҲ’\mathsf{Body}(r)^{-}. A rule rr is called a constraint when рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r)\mathsf{Head}(r) contains no atom. A program PP is called a disjunctive logic program if there is a rule rвҲҲрқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P)r\in\mathsf{Rules}(P) such that |рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r)|вүҘ2|\mathsf{Head}(r)|\geq 2 [3].

In ASP, an interpretation MM over and вҖӢP\and{P} specifies which atoms are assigned рқ—Қрқ—Ӣрқ—Һрқ–ҫ\mathsf{true}; that is, an atom aa is рқ—Қрқ—Ӣрқ—Һрқ–ҫ\mathsf{true} under MM if and only if aвҲҲMa\in M (or рқ–ҝрқ–әрқ—…рқ—Ңрқ–ҫ\mathsf{false} when aвҲүMa
ot\in M resp.). An interpretation MM satisfies a rule rr, denoted by MвҠ§rM\models r, if and only if (рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r)вҲӘрқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)вҲ’)вҲ©Mвү вҲ…(\mathsf{Head}(r)\cup\mathsf{Body}(r)^{-})\cap M
eq\emptyset or рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)+вҲ–Mвү вҲ…\mathsf{Body}(r)^{+}\setminus M
eq\emptyset. An interpretation MM is a model of PP, denoted by MвҠ§PM\models P, when вҲҖrвҲҲрқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P)MвҠ§r orall_{r\in\mathsf{Rules}(P)}M\models r. The Gelfond-Lifschitz (GL) reduct of a program PP, with respect to an interpretation MM, is defined as PM={рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r)вҶҗрқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)+|rвҲҲрқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P),рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r)вҲ’вҲ©M=вҲ…}P^{M}=\{\mathsf{Head}(r)\leftarrow\mathsf{Body}(r)^{+}|r\in\mathsf{Rules}(P),\mathsf{Body}(r)^{-}\cap M=\emptyset\} [12]. An interpretation MM is an answer set of PP if MвҠ§PM\models P and no MвҖӢВҙвҠӮMM	extprime\subset M exists such that MвҖӢВҙвҠ§PMM	extprime\models P^{M}. We denote the answer sets of program PP using the notation рқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{AS}(P).

subsections.title: Exact Answer Set Counting[18]
paragraphs: Given an ASP program PP, the exact answer set counting seeks to count the number of answer sets of PP; more formally, the problem seeks to find |рқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)||\mathsf{AS}(P)|.

subsections.title: Approximate Answer Set Counting[19]
paragraphs: Given an ASP program PP, tolerance parameter Пө\epsilon, and confidence parameter Оҙ\delta, the approximate answer set counting seeks to estimate the number of answer sets of PP with a probabilistic guarantee; more formally, the approximate answer set counters returns a count cc such that рқ–Ҝрқ—ӢвҖӢ[|рқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)|/1+ПөвүӨcвүӨ(1+Пө)Г—|рқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)|]вүҘ1вҲ’Оҙ\mathsf{Pr}[\nicefrac{{|\mathsf{AS}(P)|}}{{1+\epsilon}}\leq c\leq(1+\epsilon)\times|\mathsf{AS}(P)|]\geq 1-\delta. Our approximate answer set counter invokes a polynomial number of calls to an ASP solver.

subsections.title: ClarkвҖҷs completion
paragraphs: [6] or program completion is a technique to translate a normal program PP into a propositional formula рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P) that is related but not semantically equivalent. Specifically, for each atom aa in and вҖӢP\and{P}, we perform the following steps: Finally, рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P) is derived by logically conjoining all the previously added constraints. Literature indicates that while every answer set of PP satisfies рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P), the converse is not true [22].

1. Let r1,вҖҰ,rkвҲҲрқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P)r_{1},\ldots,r_{k}\in\mathsf{Rules}(P) such that рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r1)=вҖҰ=рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(rk)=a\mathsf{Head}(r_{1})=\ldots=\mathsf{Head}(r_{k})=a, then we add the propositional formula (aвҶ”(рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r1)вҲЁвҖҰвҲЁрқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(rk)))(a\leftrightarrow(\mathsf{Body}(r_{1})\vee\ldots\vee\mathsf{Body}(r_{k}))) to рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P).
2. Otherwise, we add the literal В¬a\neg{a} to рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P).

Let r1,вҖҰ,rkвҲҲрқ–ұрқ—Һрқ—…рқ–ҫрқ—ҢвҖӢ(P)r_{1},\ldots,r_{k}\in\mathsf{Rules}(P) such that рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(r1)=вҖҰ=рқ–§рқ–ҫрқ–әрқ–ҪвҖӢ(rk)=a\mathsf{Head}(r_{1})=\ldots=\mathsf{Head}(r_{k})=a, then we add the propositional formula (aвҶ”(рқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(r1)вҲЁвҖҰвҲЁрқ–Ўрқ—Ҳрқ–Ҫрқ—’вҖӢ(rk)))(a\leftrightarrow(\mathsf{Body}(r_{1})\vee\ldots\vee\mathsf{Body}(r_{k}))) to рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P).
Otherwise, we add the literal В¬a\neg{a} to рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P).

The decision version of normal logic programs is NP-complete; therefore, the ASP counting for normal logic programs is #P-complete [25] via a polynomial reduction [17]. Given the #P-completeness, a prominent line of work focused on ASP counting relies on translations from the ASP program to a CNF formula [22, 15, 16, 17]. Such translations often result in a large number of CNF clauses and thereby limit practical scalability for non-tight ASP programs. Eiter et al. [7] introduced TP-unfolding to break cycles and produce a tight program. They proposed an ASP counter called aspmc, that performs a treewidth-aware Clark completion from a cycle-free program to a CNF formula. Jakl, Pichler, and Woltran [14] extended the tree decomposition based approach for #SAT due to Samer and Szeider [24] to ASP and proposed a fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) algorithm for ASP counting. Fichte et al. [10, 9] revisited the FPT algorithm due to Jakl et al. [14] and developed an exact model counter, called DynASP, that performs well on instances with low treewidth. Aziz et al. [2] extended a propositional model counter to an answer set counter by integrating unfounded set detection. ASP solvers [11] can count answer set via enumeration, which is suitable for a sufficiently small number of answer sets. Kabir et al. [19] focused on lifting hashing-based techniques to ASP counting, resulting in an approximate counter, called ApproxASP, with (Оө,Оҙ)(\varepsilon,\delta)-guarantees. Kabir et al. [18] introduced an ASP counter that utilizes a sophisticated Boolean formula, termed the copy formula, which features a compact encoding.

We have already engineered two ASP counters: SharpASP [18] and ApproxASP [19]. SharpASP111https://github.com/meelgroup/SharpASP is an exact answer set counter and ApproxASP222https://github.com/meelgroup/ApproxASP2 is an approximate answer set counter.

The principal contribution of SharpASP is to design a scalable answer set counter, without a substantial increase in the size of the transformed propositional formula, particularly when addressing circular dependencies. The key idea behind a substantial reduction in the size of the transformed formula is an alternative yet correlated perspective of defining answer sets. This alternative definition formulates the answer set counting problem on a pair of Boolean formulas (F,G)(F,G), where the formula FF over-approximates the search space of answer sets, while the formula GG exploits justifications to identify answer sets correctly. We set F=рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)F=\mathsf{Comp}(P) since every answer set satisfies Clark completion. Note that рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P) overapproximates answers sets of PP. We propose another formula, named copy formula, denoted as рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Copy}(P), which comprises a set of (implicitly conjoined) implications defined as follows:
1. (type 1) for every vвҲҲрқ–«рқ– вҖӢ(P)v\in\mathsf{LA}(P), the implication vвҖӢВҙвҶ’vv\textprime\rightarrow v is in рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Copy}(P).

2. (type 2) for every rule xвҶҗa1,вҖҰak,b1,вҖҰbm,вҲјc1,вҖҰвҲјcnx\leftarrow a_{1},\ldots a_{k},b_{1},\ldots b_{m},\sim c_{1},\ldots\sim c_{n} in PP, where xвҲҲрқ–«рқ– вҖӢ(P)x\in\mathsf{LA}(P), {a1,вҖҰвҖӢak}вҠҶрқ–«рқ– вҖӢ(P)\{a_{1},\ldots a_{k}\}\subseteq\mathsf{LA}(P) and {b1,вҖҰвҖӢbm}вҲ©рқ–«рқ– вҖӢ(P)=вҲ…\{b_{1},\ldots b_{m}\}\cap\mathsf{LA}(P)=\emptyset, the implication a1вҖӢВҙвҲ§вҖҰвҖӢakвҖӢВҙвҲ§b1вҲ§вҖҰвҖӢbmвҲ§В¬c1вҲ§вҖҰвҖӢВ¬cnвҶ’xвҖӢВҙa_{1}\textprime\wedge\ldots a_{k}\textprime\wedge b_{1}\wedge\ldots b_{m}\wedge\neg{c_{1}}\wedge\ldots\neg{c_{n}}\rightarrow x\textprime is in рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Copy}(P).
3. No other implication is in рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Copy}(P).

For each satisfying assignment MвҠ§рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)M\models\mathsf{Comp}(P), we have the following observations: where рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)|M\mathsf{Copy}(P)_{|M} denotes the unit propagation of MM on рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—Ҷрқ—үвҖӢ(P)\mathsf{Comp}(P). We integrate these observations into propositional model counters to engineer an answer set counter.
вҖў if MвҲҲрқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)M\in\mathsf{AS}(P), then рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)|M=вҲ…\mathsf{Copy}(P)_{|M}=\emptyset
вҖў if MвҲүрқ– рқ–ІвҖӢ(P)M\not\in\mathsf{AS}(P), then рқ–ўрқ—Ҳрқ—үрқ—’вҖӢ(P)|Mвү вҲ…\mathsf{Copy}(P)_{|M}\neq\emptyset

Within ApproxASP, we present a scalable approach to approximate the number of answer sets. Inspired by approximate model counter ApproxMC [5], our approach is based on systematically adding parity (XOR) constraints to ASP programs to divide the search space uniformly and independently. We prove that adding random XOR constraints partitions the answer sets of an ASP program. When a randomly chosen partition is quite small, we can approximate the number of answer sets by simple multiplication. The XOR semantic in answer set programs was initiated by Everardo et al. [8]. In practice, we use a Gaussian elimination-based approach by lifting ideas from SAT to ASP and integrating them into a state-of-the-art ASP solver.

Our objective is to develop more efficient answer set counters by integrating specialized ASP counting techniques and advanced preprocessing methods. Furthermore, we are dedicated to enhancing the capabilities of SharpASP, currently limited to handling normal programs, to also support disjunctive answer set programs. In addition, we are eager to explore broader applications of ASP counting to demonstrate its versatility and potential in solving complex problems. We are also eager to extend the counting technique in other theories [21].

We implemented prototypes of both SharpASP, on top of the existing propositional model counter SharpSAT-TD (denoted as SharpASP (STD) and ApproxASP, on top of ASP solver Clingo. Finally, we empirically evaluate their performance against existing counting benchmarks used in answer set counting literature [10, 7, 2].

subsections.title: SharpASP
paragraphs: Our extensive empirical analysis of 14701470 benchmarks demonstrates significant performance gain over current state-of-the-art exact answer set counters. The result demonstrated is presented in Table 11 and the rightmost column presents the result of SharpASP. Specifically, by using SharpASP, we were able to solve 10231023 benchmarks with a PAR22 score of 33733373, whereas using prior state-of-the-art, we could only solve 869869 benchmarks with a PAR22 score of 42854285. A detailed experimental analysis revealed that the strength of SharpASP is that it spends less time in binary constraint propagation while making more decisions compared to off-the-shelf propositional model counters.

subsections.title: ApproxASP
paragraphs: Table 22 presents the result of ApproxASP with state-of-the-art answer set counters. ApproxASP performs well in disjunctive logic programs. ApproxASP solved 185185 instances among 200200 instances, while the best ASP solver clingo solved a total of 177177 instances. In addition, on normal logic programs, ApproxASP performs on par with state-of-the-art approximate model counter ApproxMC.

Model counting, an intractable problem, is classified as #P for normal programs and #co-NP [10] for disjunctive logic programs, presenting significant challenges in developing scalable answer set counters. Our observations indicate that while our engineered counters effectively scale for certain problem types, they underperform for others. The diverse applications of model counting in real-world scenarios further complicate the creation of application-specific ASP counters. Moreover, we have identified instances where existing systems outperform our SharpASP counter. Integrating strengths from these existing counters into SharpASP to enhance its scalability remains a formidable challenge.
